content.text: What it is

Capacity test automation for agentic AI applications means running scheduled, scripted load tests that verify the system can handle projected peak traffic levels while staying within defined SLA bounds. Unlike one-off performance audits, automated capacity tests run repeatedly — typically weekly in staging — and produce machine-readable pass/fail results that gate releases and feed capacity planning decisions. They answer a concrete question: can this system serve 3× the current peak load without breaching latency or error-rate SLAs?
Why it matters

Agentic AI systems face unpredictable demand spikes driven by viral product moments, marketing campaigns, or new enterprise customers onboarding. LLM inference does not scale linearly with concurrency: as token throughput approaches provider limits, latency grows non-linearly and error rates from rate-limit responses climb. Without regular capacity tests, teams discover scaling limits in production rather than staging. At that point, the options are limited and expensive: emergency capacity buys, emergency hotfixes to add queuing, or accepting degraded SLAs while the team scrambles.

Capacity tests also surface infrastructure assumptions that become wrong over time. A system designed for 200 concurrent users may have its concurrency model invalidated by a new multi-step agent feature that multiplies the number of LLM calls per user session by three. Automated capacity tests with traffic models grounded in real usage patterns catch this drift in staging before it reaches production.
Benefits
Provides objective evidence that the system can survive projected peak loads
Catches scaling regressions introduced by new model versions or feature additions
Feeds infrastructure cost modelling: knowing the saturation point enables right-sizing
Surfaces rate-limit exposure before production load manifests it
Creates a reproducible audit trail for capacity reviews with SRE and finance stakeholders
Trade-offs and limitations
Capacity tests against real LLM APIs incur significant API cost — staging tests at 3× peak can cost hundreds of dollars per run
Provider-level rate limits in staging environments may differ from production, making the test a proxy rather than a direct measurement
Traffic models become stale as user behaviour evolves; models must be refreshed quarterly or after significant product changes
Capacity tests do not predict cascade failures in downstream dependencies that share the same provider rate limits
Test duration must be long enough to reach steady state, which can mean 30–60 minute test runs that block CI if not run separately
How it works

A capacity test run follows a four-stage cycle. First, a traffic model generator produces a request schedule that reflects the production concurrency shape: ramp-up over 5 minutes, sustained peak for 20 minutes, ramp-down over 5 minutes. The peak concurrency is parameterised so the same test script runs at 1×, 2×, and 3× expected peak by changing a single environment variable.

Second, the load executor (typically k6 or Locust) sends requests according to the schedule, rotating through a corpus of representative prompts to avoid cache effects. Third, a metrics collector aggregates latency percentiles, error rates, token throughput, and LLM provider error codes (429 rate limit, 500 server error, 503 overload) into time-series data. Fourth, a pass/fail gate reads the aggregated metrics and fails the test run if any SLA threshold or error rate limit is exceeded during the sustained-peak phase.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Capacity Test Automation Pipeline: Traffic Model Generator to Load Executor to Metrics Collector to Pass/Fail Gate

content.text: The pipeline runs as a scheduled CI job (weekly) and on demand before major releases. The traffic model is versioned alongside the codebase, so changes to the user journey automatically update the load shape on the next run.
When to use it

Run capacity tests weekly in staging environments that mirror production infrastructure. Run on-demand before any release that introduces a new LLM model version, a new multi-step agent workflow, or a change to concurrency limits. Trigger immediately after any production incident caused by capacity exhaustion. Avoid running against production directly — use a shadow traffic approach or a staging environment with production-equivalent infrastructure configuration.
How to implement
Instrument production to collect the concurrency distribution, prompt length distribution, and requests-per-second patterns for a representative two-week window
Build a traffic model from this data: a JSON or Python fixture that specifies the concurrency ramp shape and the prompt corpus sampling weights
Write a k6 or Locust script that reads the traffic model and executes the load profile at configurable peak-multiplier values (1x, 2x, 3x)
Configure the metrics collector (Prometheus or k6's built-in sink) to capture P50/P90/P95 latency, error rate, and LLM 429/503 counts per time bucket
Write the pass/fail gate as a CI step that reads the summary JSON from the collector and returns exit code 1 if thresholds are violated
Schedule the full suite (all three peak multipliers) as a weekly CI workflow with results published to a dashboard
Add a required on-demand capacity test stage to the release pipeline gating major releases
Examples
Real-world example

A customer support automation team ran their first capacity test before a major enterprise customer launch. At 1× peak (100 concurrent users) all SLAs were met. At 2× peak (200 users) P95 crept to 2800 ms against a 2000 ms target and 429 errors from the LLM provider appeared at 0.8% rate. Root cause: the system was sharing a single API key pool across all concurrent requests, and the provider's per-key rate limit was being hit.

The fix — distributing load across a key pool with round-robin allocation — brought 2× peak back within SLA. The 3× peak test still failed, which led to a pre-purchase of higher rate-limit tiers before the launch. Without the automated capacity test, this would have surfaced during the enterprise onboarding itself, a much more costly moment to discover the problem.
Common mistakes
Using synthetic constant-rate load (e.g., exactly 100 req/s) instead of a bursty traffic model that reflects real user behaviour
Running capacity tests against a mock LLM instead of a real model — mock tests do not reveal rate-limit or inference-latency issues
Setting test duration too short: LLM backends under load take 2–5 minutes to fully saturate; tests shorter than 15 minutes miss steady-state behaviour
Not parameterising the peak multiplier: hardcoding 2× means teams never discover the 3× saturation point until a real traffic spike
Running capacity tests in the same CI slot as unit and integration tests — the 30-minute runtime blocks the main pipeline
Forgetting to refresh the traffic model after major new features ship, resulting in tests that no longer reflect the actual request mix
Best practices
Version the traffic model in the same repository as the service code and update it after every significant usage pattern change
Separate capacity test CI from the main test suite — run capacity tests on a weekly schedule and on-demand pre-release, not on every PR
Test at 1×, 2×, and 3× peak multiplier in a single run; require 2× pass for normal releases, 3× pass for major launches
Rotate through at least 200 distinct prompts with realistic length distributions to prevent cache effects skewing results
Publish capacity test results to a shared dashboard visible to SRE, product, and finance stakeholders
Alert on capacity test failures as P1 issues that block the release train, not as background noise
Performance and scalability

Capacity tests are themselves resource-intensive. Running a 3× peak test against a staging environment that mirrors production requires provisioning staging to match production capacity, which doubles infrastructure cost during the test window. Manage this by using on-demand infrastructure (Kubernetes node pools that scale up for the test and tear down afterward) and scheduling tests during off-peak hours. The test automation pipeline should provision and deprovision the staging environment as part of the test job.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
k6
Load generator
Excellent thresholds engine; native Prometheus export
Locust
Load generator (Python)
Better for complex session-stateful flows
Grafana k6 Cloud
Distributed load generation
Overcomes local machine limits for very high VU counts
Prometheus
Metrics collection
Scrapes k6's HTTP metrics endpoint during test
Artillery
Alternative load tool
YAML-first; good for developers unfamiliar with k6
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Automated capacity tests (this topic)
Reproducible; CI-integrated; catches regressions early
Expensive API cost at high multipliers
Manual load tests
Flexible for exploratory capacity testing
Not repeatable; knowledge loss over time
Production canary testing
Tests real traffic; no mock concerns
Risks degrading real users; slow feedback loop
Chaos engineering
Tests failure mode resilience
Complementary to capacity tests, not a replacement
Security and reliability

Capacity test credentials (API keys used to send load to staging) must be separate from production credentials and scoped to the staging environment only. Store them in CI secrets with the same least-privilege policies as production secrets. Capacity test traffic should never route to production endpoints. Use network-level controls (separate VPC, staging DNS aliases) to enforce this. Log every capacity test run with a signed audit entry that records the traffic model version, peak multiplier, and pass/fail result for compliance review.
Modeling traffic shapes for agentic workloads

Agentic AI workloads generate request patterns that differ fundamentally from traditional web APIs. A single user session in a multi-step agent workflow may trigger 5–20 LLM calls over 30–120 seconds, with tool calls interleaved. This means concurrency measurements in terms of concurrent users do not translate directly to concurrent LLM requests — each user drives a burst of sequential and occasionally parallel model calls within their session.

Traffic model accuracy depends on decomposing the user session into its constituent LLM call graph. Instrument a production sample of 1000 sessions to measure: average calls per session, parallel fan-out factor (how many calls happen simultaneously), inter-call gaps (time between sequential calls in a session), and prompt length distribution per call type. Feed these four parameters into the load generator so the synthetic traffic faithfully reproduces the concurrency amplification effect. A model that ignores this multiplier will underestimate required capacity by the session-to-LLM-call ratio — often a factor of five or more.
Measure calls per session in production: typical interactive agents generate 3–8 LLM calls per user session
Measure parallel fan-out factor: tool-calling agents often fan out 2–4 concurrent model calls during tool response processing
Include inter-call think time in the traffic model to avoid an artificially bursty load shape
Sample prompt length distributions separately for each call type (system prompt setup vs tool result summarisation vs final response)
Regenerate the traffic model automatically from production telemetry on a monthly cadence
Cost governance for capacity tests

Automated capacity tests at 3× peak against a real LLM API can consume significant token budgets. At GPT-4o prices (circa mid-2025), a 30-minute sustained test at 150 concurrent users generating 500-token prompts and 256-token responses produces approximately 150 users × 30 min × 2 req/min × 756 tokens = 6.8 million tokens per run. At $5 per million tokens, that is $34 per run. At 3× multiplier with three runs (1×, 2×, 3×), a single weekly test cycle costs roughly $200 in API charges.

Govern this cost with three controls. First, time-box tests: the 20-minute sustained-peak phase is sufficient for steady-state measurement; longer durations add cost without adding signal. Second, use a cheaper model tier for the volume load and reserve the most expensive model calls for a smaller targeted SLA benchmark. Third, set a per-run cost budget in the CI job that reads estimated token consumption from the test metrics and aborts the run with an error if it exceeds the budget before the test completes — this prevents runaway tests from an unexpected prompt length distribution.

# ci/capacity_test_cost_guard.py
"""
Reads k6 summary JSON and calculates estimated API cost.
Fails with exit code 1 if estimated cost exceeds budget.
"""
import json, sys

COST_PER_MILLION_TOKENS = 5.0  # USD, adjust per model tier
BUDGET_USD = 150.0

def estimate_cost(summary: dict) -> float:
    # k6 custom metrics: total_input_tokens and total_output_tokens
    input_tokens = summary.get("metrics", {}).get("total_input_tokens", {}).get("values", {}).get("count", 0)
    output_tokens = summary.get("metrics", {}).get("total_output_tokens", {}).get("values", {}).get("count", 0)
    total_millions = (input_tokens + output_tokens) / 1_000_000
    return total_millions * COST_PER_MILLION_TOKENS

if __name__ == "__main__":
    with open(sys.argv[1]) as f:
        summary = json.load(f)
    cost = estimate_cost(summary)
    print(f"Estimated API cost: ${cost:.2f}")
    if cost > BUDGET_USD:
        print(f"FAIL: Cost ${cost:.2f} exceeds budget ${BUDGET_USD}")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Cost within budget.")
Reporting and stakeholder communication

Capacity test results serve different audiences with different information needs. SRE and platform teams want raw metrics: percentile latencies at each load level, error rate curves, and saturation points. Engineering leads want go/no-go signals tied to release decisions. Finance and product stakeholders want a one-sentence summary: 'System can handle 3× projected peak launch traffic within SLA' or 'System fails at 2× peak; requires scaling action before launch.'

Automate the report generation so a human does not need to interpret raw k6 output to make a release decision. Structure the report as a capacity card: the date, the model version tested, the infrastructure configuration, the traffic model version, and a table of load levels with pass/fail results for each SLA criterion. Publish this card as a CI artifact and link it in the release checklist. When a capacity test fails, the card must also include a root-cause hypothesis (latency breach, rate-limit breach, or error-rate breach) to guide the remediation triage.
Automate capacity card generation as a CI artifact — never require manual interpretation of raw metrics
Include: date, model version, infra config, traffic model version, pass/fail table by load level
Link the capacity card in the release checklist as a required attachment before launch sign-off
Failed runs must include root-cause classification: latency breach, rate-limit 429s, or 5xx errors
Archive capacity cards for 12 months for compliance and retrospective capacity planning
Integrating capacity tests with infrastructure autoscaling

Capacity tests should not only measure whether the system survives peak load — they should also validate that autoscaling responds correctly and in time. A system configured with Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) might handle 3× peak in theory, but if the autoscaler takes 8 minutes to provision new pods and the load ramp-up happens in 5 minutes, real users experience degraded service for 3 minutes before the scaling catches up. The capacity test must model the ramp-up rate as a stress test of the autoscaler, not just the steady-state capacity.

Instrument the capacity test to record pod count over time alongside request latency. A correctly tuned autoscaler should begin scaling within 60–90 seconds of the ramp-up start and reach target replica count before the sustained-peak phase begins. If pods are still provisioning when the sustained phase starts, the scale-up is too slow and the HPA target utilisation threshold needs lowering or the scale-up stabilisation window needs shortening.
Record pod count as a time-series metric during the capacity test to verify autoscaler responsiveness
Model ramp-up rate to match production burst patterns — 5-minute ramps are common for marketing campaign spikes
Verify that new replicas become ready before the sustained peak phase begins
Test scale-down: confirm pods terminate cleanly without in-flight requests being dropped
Validate that scale-down does not happen prematurely during brief inter-burst lulls in the traffic shape
Key takeaways
Automate capacity tests to run weekly and before major releases; treat failures as release blockers
Use a production-derived traffic model with realistic prompt lengths and concurrency shapes
Test at 1×, 2×, and 3× expected peak to understand headroom and saturation points
Run against real LLM APIs in staging — mock-based tests cannot reveal rate-limit or inference-latency issues
Keep capacity test CI separate from the main test pipeline to avoid blocking PRs with 30-minute test runs
Refresh the traffic model quarterly or after major feature launches to prevent model staleness
